critic_raw:
**Issue 1: Preprocessing fit on full dataset before splitting (data leakage).** Section 4 states: "preprocessing transformers (tokenizer, text normalizer, vocabulary builder) are fit on the entire dataset before splitting." For a ClinicalBERT/BioBERT fine-tuning experiment on 100,000 patient notes, fitting the vocabulary and tokenizer on the full dataset exposes training to test-period clinical terminology. Additionally, structured feature scaling is also noted as fit on full data — any information from the test-period distribution of lab values, LOS, or comorbidity counts feeds into the normalization applied during training.

**Issue 2: Explicit test set contamination via 'cross-verification.'** Section 6 states: "a final cross-verification on the test set is conducted to confirm the chosen hyperparameter configuration maintains its performance edge." And: "This step acts as a safeguard against getting trapped in local optima during tuning." This is test-set peeking rationalized as a safeguard. Once the test set is consulted to "confirm performance edge," the holdout guarantee is broken — the practitioner now has information about test-set performance that can guide subsequent decisions, even informally.

**Issue 3: Single complication label assignment loses clinical information and creates arbitrary label assignment.** Section 2 specifies: "Each patient receives a single complication class label (the primary or most severe complication)." Patients frequently experience multiple post-discharge complications. Assigning only the most severe means: (a) the model is never trained to recognize co-occurring complications, (b) the severity ranking used to determine 'primary' may not align across patients (e.g., is pneumonia more severe than UTI?), and (c) patients with two moderate complications are treated identically to patients with one severe complication. This label construction choice directly impacts macro-F1 on minority classes.

**Issue 4: The temporal split confound claim is incorrect.** Section 8 states: "Training, validation, and test sets span the same time period" as a control for documentation quality variation over time. This is false by construction — the temporal split places training in the earliest 22 months, validation in the middle 7 months, and test in the most recent 7 months. These explicitly do not span the same time period. The proposed control does not exist.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers including tokenizer, vocabulary builder, and structured feature scalers are fit on the full dataset before splitting, leaking test-period clinical terminology distributions and structured feature statistics into training-time preprocessing.

Section 6 explicitly conducts 'final cross-verification on the test set' after hyperparameter selection to confirm performance edge — this is rationalized test-set peeking that violates holdout integrity regardless of the stated safeguard framing.

Assigning each patient a single 'most severe' complication label discards multi-complication cases, relies on an unspecified severity ranking, and causes patients with two moderate complications to be labeled identically to single severe cases, directly distorting macro-F1 estimates for minority complication classes.

The design claims temporal train/val/test sets 'span the same time period' as a documentation quality control, but the temporal split explicitly places training in the earliest 22 months and test in the most recent 7 months — these do not span the same period, and the stated confound control does not exist.
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing transformers including tokenizer, vocabulary builder, and structured feature scalers are fit on the full dataset before splitting, leaking test-period clinical terminology distributions and structured feature statistics into training-time preprocessing.

Section 6 explicitly conducts 'final cross-verification on the test set' after hyperparameter selection to confirm performance edge — this is rationalized test-set peeking that violates holdout integrity regardless of the stated safeguard framing.

Assigning each patient a single 'most severe' complication label discards multi-complication cases, relies on an unspecified severity ranking, and causes patients with two moderate complications to be labeled identically to single severe cases, directly distorting macro-F1 estimates for minority complication classes.

The design claims temporal train/val/test sets 'span the same time period' as a documentation quality control, but the temporal split explicitly places training in the earliest 22 months and test in the most recent 7 months — these do not span the same period, and the stated confound control does not exist.
